title: Foreign policy expert talks about rivalry between Iran and Israel
author: ABC News
fullText: And joining me now is Trita Parcy. He's the executive vice president of the Quincy Institute, a foreign policy think tank based here in DC. He's also the author of Treacherous Alliance: The Secret Dealings of Israel, Iran, and the US. Trita, thank you so much for your time today. Uh, but you just heard Yeah, thanks for joining us. You just heard President Trump say that Netanyahu did not pull the US into this conflict, though he might have forced Israel's hand. Uh what's your reaction to that? >> I think what we're seeing here is that the plan that Trump had for this war has come crashing down. He I think had the expectation that after taking out the Iranian supreme leader uh on Saturday that the Iranians essentially would have a regime implosion and by Sunday evening he would be able to declare victory long before the markets open on Monday morning. That plan I think was never likely to succeed. We're now in a completely different situation in which increasingly the Iranians are the ones that are determining the geography of this war, the length and the scope of this war. And as a result, Trump is now struggling to come up with the most convincing justifications for this decision, the most convincing goals for this um uh decision to go to war. And in that comes then obviously these inconvenient questions about how this decision came about in the first place and what was the role of the Israelis. What Marco Rubio said creates a significant problem for the administration because it reveals that they allowed Israel to determine when the United States would be at war. Even though Israel's actions would put US troops at risk, instead of saying no to Israel, the United States decided to go for war. which means that this was not the uh the option of last resort, but that there were other ways that this war could have been prevented uh and that US u servicemen and women's lives could have been saved. >> Well, and and kind of pointing out those order of operations, if you will. I I want to get back to your book because really it studied the relationship between these three countries, the US, Iran, and Israel from 1948 until now. I'm curious, man, what stands out to you most about these shifting dynamics? >> The most important uh point, I think, is that these shifting dynamics are primarily driven by geopolitical reasons rather than ideological or religious reasons. But what is most important for the last 30 years is that the Israelis have been adamant about making sure that relations between Iran and the United States do not improve. That you don't have a resolution of US Iran tensions because the United the Israelis need the United States to be squarely on the side of Israel in this geopolitical rivalry that they have with Iran. And Netanyahu has more than any other prime minister pushed the United States to go to war with Iran. and he has done everything he could successfully to sabotage the diplomacy that have taken place in the past, including the successful diplomacy that had been taking place that led to Obama's nuclear deal in 2015. Netanyahu is on record in Israel, saying that he is the reason why Trump walked out of that deal. >> One other thing that I want to point out, President Trump today said that most of the people he had in mind are dead when he was asked about the fe future of Iran's leadership. uh how do you see this power struggle playing out? >> I think that uh the Iranians are faced with some very very significant challenges because of not only the uh assassination of the Supreme Leader but that several other key individuals were killed as well. However, I think it's important to understand the Supreme Leader had already been removed from the operational decision making uh for this war. This happened already back in June when the Israelis attacked. And this is part of the reason why we have not seen any indication that the assassination of the supreme leader led to the Iranians um uh losing control over uh command and control or that their military would would be in a position of not knowing what to do. So it while it definitely creates significant problems for uh the theocracy, it has not and probably will not have a profound impact on the war making at this point, which is of course the key issue for the Trump administration. >> All right, Trina Parcy Forest, thank you so much. Thank you for having
